one_line_takeaway: A first deployment-scale audit of 91 public vision-focused MCP servers plus a released benchmark/validator suite, finding that 78% show cross-tool schema misalignment, 89% use untyped tool connections, and 41% permit privilege escalation — evidence that MCP's vision orchestration failures are protocol-level, not model-level.
key_finding: Vision MCP deployments combine strong compositional potential with pervasive protocol-level fragility. Headline validator detection rates: schema misalignment across tools 78.0% (CI 68.9-85.2), coordinate-convention errors 24.6% (CI 17.5-33.4), mask-image dimensional mismatches 17.3% (CI 11.5-25.2), and a mean of 33.8 memory-scope warnings per 100 executions for deployments with persistent visual state. Security probes (N=47) found untyped tool connections in 89.0% (CI 76.80-95.19) and privilege-escalation/data-leakage risk in 41.0% (CI 28.02-55.37). Only 8 of 91 servers implement any post-invocation runtime output validation, and segmentation outputs appear in five mutually incompatible formats. The authors argue these are systemic protocol-level deficiencies (schema semantics, memory scoping, execution traceability) rather than isolated tool bugs.
core_contribution: An empirical, protocol-level audit of the Model Context Protocol (MCP) as used to orchestrate computer-vision tool workflows. The authors filter a large public MCP server corpus down to 91 vision-centric servers, annotate each along nine axes of 'compositional fidelity', and build an executable benchmark plus a validator suite that detects and categorizes protocol violations (schema-format divergence, coordinate-convention mismatches, missing runtime validation, unscoped/stale memory, and security weaknesses such as privilege escalation and untyped tool connections). They quantify the prevalence of these failures, present a taxonomy of four orchestration patterns and eight security threat vectors, run five deployment case studies, and propose (as reference prototypes in a testbed) protocol extensions: semantically grounded schemas (semantic_role/modality/coordinate_system fields), protocol-native versioned visual memory, and runtime validator contracts. The paper explicitly frames itself as an empirical audit, NOT a new algorithm or theory. Note that several failure categories cited in prose (semantic-role standardization, memory hygiene, canonical toolchain templates) draw on related work; the paper's own contribution is the audit + benchmark/validator suite + extension proposals, not the underlying MCP primitives (which are Anthropic's) or the MCPServerCorpus dataset (which is cited work, ref [22]).
threat_model: Stated explicitly in the 'Threat Model and Security Assumptions' paragraph. The adversary is modeled as a benign agent developer or integrator operating WITHIN declared protocol boundaries (i.e., not a fully external attacker). Tools are treated as untrusted binaries; agents build plans from public schema metadata. In scope: silent tool misuse, type mismatches, privilege escalation across memory scopes, improper fallback paths, and attacks via undocumented fields or persistent memory. The eight-vector taxonomy (Table 3) covers prompt injection via image metadata, schema bypass, remote code execution through eval/os.system shell wrappers, privilege escalation via overpermissive registries, stale memory access, untracked provenance, cross-tool leakage, and command injection via type coercion. Explicitly OUT of scope: model inversion and training-time poisoning.
limitations: Authors' own: corpus is public-only, so research prototypes are overrepresented and production/enterprise/safety-critical systems underrepresented, limiting generalizability of every prevalence rate; the 91 servers are a filtered subset requiring interpretive judgment with NO inter-rater agreement measured; extensions/validators are testbed prototypes never deployed or load-tested; security analysis covers only 47 servers; several attack scenarios lack documented large-scale real-world exploits; the rapidly evolving MCP ecosystem may invalidate the snapshot; no comparison against alternative orchestration frameworks. Reviewer-noted: the paper contains internal numeric inconsistencies — the abstract and Section 5 call the audited set '91 vision-centric servers' while the introduction says 46 vision deployments were identified among 91 servers; likewise 'schema misalignment' is 78.0% in Table 2/abstract but 'schema format divergence' is 62% in Table 4, and coordinate conventions are '24.6% undeclared' in Table 4 yet '87% undeclared coordinate formats/conventions' in Table 1 and prose. These are used somewhat interchangeably and are not reconciled.

datasets_used: MCPServerCorpus (ref [22]) — reported as 13,942 publicly registered MCP deployments, filtered to 91 vision-centric servers
47-server subset of the corpus used for controlled security probes
Five case-study deployments: ParaView-MCP, SUMO+YOLO-MCP (SUMO traffic sim + YOLOv5), ALITA, FHIR-MCP (DICOM + HL7), Blender-RCP
quantitative_results: Memory-scope warnings: mean 33.8 per 100 executions (95% CI 28.4-39.9) [Table 2]
Privilege escalation / leakage via tool binding: 41.0% (95% CI 31.4-51.3 in Table 2; 28.02-55.37 in Table 3/abstract)

Table 4 prevalence: schema format divergence 62%, no runtime schema validation 89%, undeclared coordinate conventions 87%, out-of-band bridging scripts 41%, undocumented memory retention 55%, declared compositional fallbacks only 9%
Only 8 of 91 servers implement post-invocation output validation
Orchestration pattern mix: 37% static composition, 29% retrieval-augmented, 21% dynamic orchestration, 13% multi-agent

Case studies: SUMO+YOLO-MCP 27.6% of 134 invocation pairs had projection conflicts; ALITA 18.4% of 143 toolchains produced malformed responses; FHIR-MCP 14.9% of 108 captioning outputs had scale mismatches (hallucinated diagnoses/false negatives); Blender-RCP 22 of 97 compositions had orphaned refs/cache conflicts, up to 1.3 GB state per session; ParaView-MCP latency peaks >2.3 s
baselines_compared: None — the paper explicitly states comparative analysis against alternative orchestration frameworks was NOT conducted and is left as future work. It is an audit, not a comparative benchmark of competing methods.
